meta.title: BMW N13/N20 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost

hero.h1: BMW N13/N20 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The BMW N13 and N20 are 1.6- and 2.0-litre turbo petrol fours — responsive and efficient, but timing‑chain guide failure is a well‑known weakness. Backed by 800+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [ESTIMATED].

verdictRating.oneLineVerdict: "The N13/N20 petrols are responsive and efficient — but timing‑chain guide failure can occur without warning on higher‑mileage units — insist on documented chain history." (Clause 1: 7 words. Clause 2: 11 words. Clause 3: 6 words. Total: 24 words.)

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an N13/N20 typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [EM-QUOTE].

cta.label: → Compare BMW N13/N20 engine prices from UK specialists

items.question: How much does a BMW N13/N20 engine replacement cost?
items.answer: A reconditioned N13 or N20 typically costs £2,500–£4,000 supply only [EM-QUOTE], varying by variant and displacement. Fitting adds £700–£1,400 depending on model and labour rates.

items.question: Is the BMW N13/N20 a reliable engine?
items.answer: The N13 and N20 earn 3.0★ out of 5 based on UK specialist reports — they are responsive and efficient, but timing‑chain guide wear is a well‑known issue, especially on higher‑mileage examples.

items.question: What is the most common BMW N13/N20 failure?
items.answer: Timing‑chain guide failure is the most common issue — guides can break, causing the chain to jump and potentially destroying the valvetrain. VANOS solenoid issues and oil leaks from the oil filter housing are also frequent.

items.question: Which BMW models use the N13/N20?
items.answer: The N13 powers the F20/F21 1 Series (114i–120i), while the N20 fits the F30/F31 3 Series, F10 5 Series, F25 X3, E84 X1, F32 4 Series, and E89 Z4 — covering a wide range of BMW petrol models from 2011–2017.

items.question: How long does the BMW N13/N20 last?
items.answer: With diligent maintenance and proactive timing‑chain guide replacement at 70,000–80,000 miles, these engines can reach 150,000–200,000 miles. Neglect can lead to catastrophic failure much earlier.
